claimed_contribution: The paper claims to provide a marketing-focused account of elections by jointly examining candidate appearance, party brand image, campaign spending, and negative advertising.

The authors explicitly claim to extend prior appearance research by decomposing broad competence into distinct visual inferences, especially competence versus intelligence, and showing that these traits have party-contingent effects.
The paper claims that interactions between candidate appearance and party brand alter advertising effects, particularly the effectiveness of negative advertising.
context: The substantive setting is U.S. congressional elections, with emphasis on two-party competition between Democrats and Republicans. The evidence combines controlled evaluations of candidate photos and ad stimuli with analyses of election outcomes and campaign advertising activity.
design_and_data: The authors run multiple lab/online studies and a field analysis. They first build a face-trait database from 153 U.S. House races (2000, 2002, 2004), using candidate photos and participant ratings of relative competence, intelligence, trustworthiness, and likeability, plus party-guess judgments; they also run a 130-person survey to validate party-level competence-versus-intelligence associations. Study 1 (n = 327; 2 x 2 x 2 between-subjects) tests appearance x party effects on candidate evaluations, and Study 2 (n = 291; 2 x 2 x 2) tests appearance x party under negative advertising; follow-ups test stereotype-based alternatives. Study 3 regresses incumbent vote share on appearance measures, spending and negative-tone measures (Federal Election Commission and Wisconsin Advertising Project), and district partisanship (Cook index), and Study 4 tests whether visual party stereotypicality predicts outcomes.
main_findings: Candidate evaluations depended on an interaction between appearance and party label: intelligent-looking candidates were favored more when labeled Democrat, and competent-looking candidates were favored more when labeled Republican.

In congressional election data, party-contingent trait advantages predicted vote share: Democrats benefited from looking more intelligent than competent, while Republicans benefited from looking more competent than intelligent.
Negative advertising effects were moderated by appearance-party trait fit, and matching a broad visual party stereotype did not consistently explain candidate advantage.
one_line_summary: Using experiments and U.S. House election data, the paper shows that appearance-based trait inferences influence electoral outcomes mainly when they match party-level trait associations, with important implications for negative advertising effects.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior work that linked facially inferred competence to election success by separating competence from intelligence and modeling how these inferences interact with party brand associations and advertising strategy.
research_gap: Prior studies had examined candidate appearance, campaign spending, and negative advertising largely in isolation, and appearance work emphasized aggregate competence rather than finer trait distinctions. The paper also notes a lack of empirical evidence on whether party brands are differentially associated with competence versus intelligence and whether trait-party match, rather than visual stereotype match, drives outcomes.
research_question: How do personality trait inferences from candidate appearance interact with party brand associations to shape voter preference and congressional vote share? Do these appearance-party interactions also change the effects of campaign spending and especially negative advertising, and are the effects better explained by trait-party matching or by matching a visual party stereotype?
